description: Complete installation guide and project record for a 7-device AXIS/OpenEye security system at ABC Supply Site 457, Plainfield, NJ. Covers network design, cable infrastructure, server setup, camera configuration, P2MP Unify wireless, enrollment, and commissioning across five days.

content.value: ABC Supply Site 457 is a warehouse and distribution facility in Plainfield, NJ. Full security system installation: six structured cable runs, a network rack, an OpenEye VMS server, six AXIS cameras across three models, an AXIS D4100-VE Mk II network strobe siren, and a P2MP Unify wireless backhaul for two pole-mounted cameras. Work ran May 4–8, 2026 across five days.

items: Day 1 — May 4 (10 hrs): Cable infrastructure and rack
Day 2 — May 5 (10 hrs): Server setup, four cameras installed
Day 3 — May 6 (10 hrs): Remaining cameras, P2MP Unify, enrollment
Day 4 — May 7 (6 hrs): Network reprogramming to customer specs, OpenEye registration, corridor view removal
Day 5 — May 8 (5 hrs): NanoStation IPs finalized, 3 cameras re-aimed, system complete

sections.summary: Scope, site description, and project timeline at a glance.
sections.title: Project Overview

content.value: All devices share a single subnet. Cameras terminate at the Telaid head end (AXIS) switch. The OpenEye server's client port connects to the head end switch, which uplinks to the Jupiter customer switch. The camera port on the server is not used. P2MP Unify provides wireless backhaul for both pole-mounted cameras.

items: Subnet: 10.19.157.192/27 — mask 255.255.255.224, gateway 10.19.157.193 (updated Day 4 to customer spec — see Final Network State)
Head end (AXIS) switch: 10.19.157.199 — all cameras connect here
OpenEye VMS server: 10.19.157.194 — client port → head end switch → Port 38, Jupiter customer switch
NanoBeam (AP): 10.19.157.200 — updated Day 4
NanoStation near pole: 10.19.157.201 — updated Day 5
NanoStation far pole: 10.19.157.202 — updated Day 5

sections.summary: Subnet, device addressing, switch topology, and P2MP wireless layout.
sections.title: Network Design

content.value: Arrived on site and ran all six cable pathways through the building per the floor plan before the server arrived. Pulling all cables on Day 1 kept the rest of the install clean — no cable work competing with camera mounting or server configuration.

items: 1. Review the cable pathway floor plan before touching anything. Identify rack room, each camera location, and the route between them.
2. Start all runs from the rack room. Label each cable at both ends before pulling — use camera number or location (e.g., CAM1-MAIN, CAM2-FRONT).
3. Pull runs in order: warehouse → floor storage → pulling area → tasing area → pole locations. Six runs total.
4. Dress and terminate at the rack end. Leave service loops at each camera end — minimum 3 ft.
5. Install the network rack. Patch panel, switch, and PoE injectors go in before the server arrives.
6. Do not power anything on until the server is racked and the switch is configured.

sections.summary: All six cable runs pulled and dressed. Network rack installed. Server not yet delivered.
sections.title: Day 1 — Cable Infrastructure

content.value: Server arrived in the afternoon on Day 2. Racked it immediately and ran through initial OpenEye Web Services setup before starting camera installation. Getting the server online first means each camera can be tested against it as it goes in.

items: 1. Rack the server. Connect client port to the head end switch — do not use the camera port.
2. Power on and allow OpenEye Web Services (OWS) to initialize. Browse to the server IP from a laptop on the same subnet.
3. Log in (admin / Telaid26). Complete the initial setup wizard — set time zone, recording defaults, and storage allocation.
4. Set recording defaults: motion-triggered + Always, Zipstream Medium, Dynamic GOP, 25 fps, compression 30, 30-day retention.
5. Begin camera installation once the server is confirmed online. Address each camera before connecting it to the head end switch (see Camera Network Configuration).
6. Four cameras were installed before end of Day 2.

sections.summary: OpenEye VMS server racked and initialized. Four cameras installed before end of day.
sections.title: Day 2 — Server Setup and Initial Cameras

content.value: Day 3 completed the physical installation, brought up the P2MP wireless backhaul, and enrolled all seven devices. By end of day the system was live and recording.

items: 1. Complete installation of all remaining cameras and the strobe siren.
2. Configure the NanoBeam AP and both NanoStations before connecting the pole cameras (see P2MP Unify sections below).
3. Confirm wireless link on both stations — check signal strength and TX/RX throughput before relying on it for camera traffic.
4. Enroll all seven devices in OpenEye (see Camera Enrollment section).
5. Aim each camera per the design headings. Confirm live view in OpenEye before locking the mount.
6. Verify recording is active on all cameras before leaving site.

sections.summary: All cameras installed. P2MP Unify configured and linked. All seven devices enrolled in OpenEye.
sections.title: Day 3 — Remaining Cameras, P2MP Unify, and Enrollment

content.value: Do this for each camera before it goes on the main switch. A laptop and a PoE injector are all you need. Address cameras in order so you always know what's what.

items: 1. Connect the camera to a PoE injector. Connect your laptop to the LAN port of the injector.
2. Use AXIS IP Utility or AXIS Device Manager to discover the camera. It will appear at its default IP (typically 192.168.0.90) or a DHCP address.
3. Open a browser and navigate to the camera's current IP. Log in: root / pass.
4. Go to System > Network > IPv4.
5. Switch from DHCP to Static.
6. Enter the IP for this camera (see Device Inventory), mask 255.255.255.224, gateway 10.19.157.193.
7. Click Save. Camera reboots on its new address.
8. Browse to the new IP to confirm it is reachable, then connect it to the head end switch.

content.caption: AXIS camera — System > Network > IPv4 — static IP fields
AXIS camera — confirmation after saving network settings

sections.summary: How to assign a static IP to each AXIS camera before connecting it to the head end switch.
sections.title: Camera Network Configuration

content.value: The NanoBeam is the AP. Configure it first — the stations cannot associate until it is broadcasting. Mount it with line of sight to both poles before powering on.

items: 1. Connect the NanoBeam to a PoE injector. Connect your laptop to the LAN port of the injector.
2. Set your laptop to a static IP on 192.168.1.x (e.g., 192.168.1.10). NanoBeam default: 192.168.1.20.
3. Browse to https://192.168.1.20. Accept the self-signed cert. Log in: ubnt / ubnt.
4. Wireless tab — Wireless Mode: Access Point. SSID: abc_cctv. Security: WPA2-AES. Passphrase: Telaid26. Channel width: 20 MHz or Auto.
5. Network tab — Network Mode: Bridge. IP Address: 10.19.157.200. Mask: 255.255.255.224. Gateway: 10.19.157.193.
6. Click Change, then Apply. NanoBeam reboots on the new IP.
7. Set your laptop back to DHCP. Browse to 10.19.157.200 to confirm it is reachable and the SSID is broadcasting.

content.caption: NanoBeam — Wireless tab: AP mode, SSID abc_cctv, WPA2-AES selected
NanoBeam — Network tab: static IP 10.19.157.200, mask and gateway configured

sections.summary: Configure the NanoBeam as the central wireless AP for both pole camera stations.
sections.title: P2MP Unify — NanoBeam Configuration (Access Point)

content.value: Each NanoStation mounts on a camera pole and backhauls that pole's AXIS P3737-PLE camera to the rack. Configure one at a time — near pole first (.201), far pole second (.202). The process is identical for both.

items: 1. Connect the NanoStation to a PoE injector. Connect your laptop to the LAN port.
2. Set your laptop to 192.168.1.x. Browse to https://192.168.1.20. Log in: ubnt / ubnt.
3. Wireless tab — Wireless Mode: Station. Click Select under SSID and scan for networks.
4. Select abc_cctv from scan results. Enter passphrase: Telaid26.
5. Network tab — Network Mode: Bridge. IP: 10.19.157.201 (near pole) or 10.19.157.202 (far pole). Mask: 255.255.255.224. Gateway: 10.19.157.193.
6. Click Change, then Apply. Station reboots and associates with the NanoBeam.
7. Browse to the station's new IP. Main tab — confirm Signal Strength (dBm) and TX/RX throughput are visible.
8. Connect the pole camera to the LAN port of the PoE injector serving the NanoStation.

content.caption: NanoStation — Wireless tab: Station mode, abc_cctv selected from scan results
NanoStation — Network tab: static IP (.201 or .202), mask and gateway set
NanoStation — Main tab after association: signal strength and throughput confirmation

sections.summary: Configure each NanoStation as a wireless station connecting back to the NanoBeam. Repeat for both poles.
sections.title: P2MP Unify — NanoStation Configuration (Station / CPE)

content.value: Enroll cameras after all devices are reachable on the subnet. The OpenEye server discovers AXIS cameras via ONVIF — you can scan or add manually. Set recording parameters per camera as you go.

items: 1. Browse to the OpenEye server: http://10.19.157.194. Log in: admin / Telaid26.
2. Navigate to Cameras > Add Camera. Run auto-discovery scan or enter each camera IP manually.
3. For each camera, enter credentials: root / pass. Confirm model and live stream appear.
4. Set recording schedule: Motion-triggered + Always. Zipstream: Medium. Dynamic GOP: enabled. 25 fps, compression 30, 30-day retention.
5. Repeat for all seven devices. Verify live stream for each before moving on.
6. Confirm recording indicators are active on all camera tiles before leaving the server interface.

sections.summary: Add all seven devices to the OpenEye server once they are powered, addressed, and on the head end switch.
sections.title: Camera Enrollment in OpenEye VMS

content.value: Model: AXIS M4318-PLVE. IP: 10.19.157.205. Mount: Ceiling. Resolution: 2992×2992. Estimated bandwidth: 7.00 Mbit/s. Estimated storage: 491 GB/30 days. Focal length: 1.2 mm. Height: ~10 ft. Heading: 180°. Rotation: 0°. Pixel density: ~41 px/ft at 23 ft.
Note: This camera was relocated during the installation. The SD card is missing and must be installed before local storage is available on this device.

sections.summary: Network strobe siren. IP .211. Wall-mounted in the tasing area. Added to scope as a late change.
sections.title: Device 7 — AXIS D4100-VE Mk II — Strobe Siren

content.value: Day 4 was a follow-up visit to implement customer-requested network changes and close out the OpenEye registration.

items: Reprogrammed all devices with customer-specified subnet mask, gateway, server IP, switch IP, and NanoBeam IP.
Set all cameras to 0° rotation — corridor view removed from any cameras that had it enabled.
Completed OpenEye server registration with OpenEye cloud — remote management and support access now active.
NanoStation IPs not yet updated this visit — carried to Day 5.

sections.summary: All devices reprogrammed to customer-specified network settings. OpenEye registration completed. Corridor views removed.
sections.title: Day 4 — Network Reprogramming and OpenEye Registration

content.value: Day 5 closed out all remaining open items except the SD card.

items: Updated both NanoStation IPs to customer-specified addresses (.201 and .202 in the new network scheme).
Completed physical aim adjustments on three cameras — all cameras now at 0° rotation and aimed per customer requirements.
All devices across the system are correctly addressed and aligned.

sections.summary: NanoStation IPs updated to customer spec. Three cameras physically re-aimed. System complete.
sections.title: Day 5 — NanoStation IPs and Final Camera Aiming
